name: Discovery Deficiency and Meet-and-Confer Tracker
summary: This skill guides an LLM to build and maintain a litigation-grade discovery deficiency and meet-and-confer tracker designed for conversion into court-ready charts, declarations, joint letters, or separate statements. It emphasizes primary-source quotation of discovery requests and responses, a legally grounded deficiency taxonomy tied to the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure and their state equivalents, detailed conferral logging with evidentiary rigor, deadline control, sanctions assessment, and dual reporting views for clients and courts. The skill incorporates ethical guardrails around competence, candor, confidentiality, and mandatory anti-hallucination requirements for all legal citations.
content:
# Discovery Deficiency and Meet-and-Confer Tracker

## 1. Opening Context

This skill produces a litigation-grade discovery deficiency and meet-and-confer tracker that a case team can rely on to manage the iterative process of curing incomplete, evasive, or bad-faith discovery responses. The tracker is more than a project-management artifact: when built correctly, it becomes the factual backbone for motion practice—most commonly a motion to compel under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 37 or its state equivalents, a request for sanctions, or a request for an informal discovery conference—and a contemporaneous record demonstrating that counsel satisfied procedural prerequisites to seek court relief. When built poorly, teams miss deadlines, lose credibility before the court, fail to preserve issues for compulsion, or invite arguments that counsel did not confer in good faith.

Professional standards governing this workflow include the applicable civil procedure rules on discovery scope and responses, supplementation, and motion practice; the court's local meet-and-confer requirements; and attorneys' ethical duties of competence, diligence, candor, and fairness in discovery. In federal cases, the core baseline is found in Federal Rules of Civil Procedure 26 through 37, including the scope and proportionality principles of Rule 26(b)(1), the duty to supplement under Rule 26(e), response obligations under Rules 33 and 34, the privilege-disclosure requirements of Rule 26(b)(5), and the motion-to-compel and sanctions frameworks of Rule 37. Because meet-and-confer practices and judicial expectations vary sharply by forum and even by individual judge, this skill is designed so that the tracker can be readily converted into court-facing declarations, joint discovery letters, or separate statements without recreating the record later.

## 2. Understanding the Context and Purpose

A discovery deficiency tracker exists to operationalize the litigation team's strategy for turning deficient responses into usable evidence, admissions, or at least a clean record for compelling production. Discovery disputes rarely resolve in a single exchange; they move through cycles of identifying deficiencies, articulating them with enough specificity to make a credible demand, receiving and evaluating opposing justifications, meeting and conferring to narrow issues, and escalating only those disputes that matter to the case narrative and dispositive motions. The tracker is the control system for that cycle.

Strategically, the tracker serves three audiences and four functions. For the internal legal team, it identifies gaps in the evidence needed for summary judgment, class certification, expert reports, or trial and helps the team prioritize: not every deficiency is worth a motion to compel, and not every objection is worth fighting if another path exists (such as a third-party subpoena, stipulation, targeted follow-up request, or deposition questioning). For opposing counsel, the deficiency articulations serve as formal notice that sets the clock for a cure period and signals preparedness for court intervention. For the court, the tracker provides a clear, chronological, and organized record of the moving party's efforts to resolve the dispute without judicial involvement, demonstrating specificity and reasonableness.

The tracker's four core functions are: prioritization of discovery fights by litigation value; leverage preservation by capturing commitments, narrowing agreements, and production dates to prevent "we never agreed to that" disputes; credibility maintenance by showing the court precise, issue-by-issue conferral rather than generalized complaints; and waiver prevention by tracking external deadlines tied to service of discovery responses or discovery cut-offs, missing which can forfeit the ability to compel.

Scope boundary: this skill manages deficiency logging, meet-and-confer tracking, and escalation readiness. It does not draft the motion to compel itself, prepare deposition outlines, or design the overall discovery plan, though it should interface with those workflows.

## 3. Prerequisites and Information Gathering

Before building or updating the tracker, the executing LLM must collect and review the core record of the disputed discovery. If any of the following inputs are missing—particularly the exact request text, exact response text, or applicable deadlines—the LLM must pause and prompt the user to provide them before generating a court-reporting-ready tracker. Otherwise, it should clearly label any entries as provisional and requiring attorney completion.

The required inputs are: (a) the served discovery requests at issue (interrogatories, requests for production, requests for admission) with full text including identifiers, definitions, and instructions; (b) the complete written responses and objections as served, including any verifications and general objections; (c) any produced documents or production logs tied to those responses, with Bates ranges; (d) all supplementation served to date (amended responses, supplemental productions, privilege logs, clawback notices); (e) all meet-and-confer communications (emails, letters, call notes) and any court filings or orders relating to discovery; (f) the operative protective order, ESI order, clawback agreement, and confidentiality designations; (g) the scheduling order, discovery cut-off, motion cut-off, and any local rule deadlines for discovery motions; and (h) the governing procedural rules and judge-specific procedures for discovery disputes, confirmed by checking the court's local rules page and the assigned judge's standing order or individual rules.

The LLM should also elicit strategic context needed to triage issues: the key claims and defenses and the elements the disputed discovery relates to; what dispositive motion or expert deadlines are driving urgency; what categories of documents are most valuable; whether the client has constraints (budget, sensitivity, time); and whether there are parallel sources for the information.

## 4. Step-by-Step Process

### Step 1: Define the Tracker's Role, Audience, and Conversion Target

Begin by deciding what the tracker must ultimately become, because that dictates the level of detail and vocabulary used. In many courts, discovery disputes are resolved through an informal conference, joint letter, or chart before motion practice; other courts require a formal motion accompanied by a certification or declaration describing good-faith conferral efforts. Some courts, such as many in the Southern District of New York, require a pre-motion letter of three pages or less. Others, like California state courts, mandate a "Separate Statement" under California Rule of Court 3.1345 that must be self-contained—meaning every request and response must be reproduced in full rather than incorporated by reference. The tracker should be structured so it can be converted with minimal editing into whichever format the forum requires: a request-by-request chart, a joint letter, or a separate statement.

This step matters because the highest-risk failure mode is having a spreadsheet that is internally useful but unusable as evidence of conferral efforts. Courts care about specificity: a motion to compel is not an invitation to relitigate the entire discovery set. The tracker should therefore be granular at the level of each request number (e.g., RFP No. 12), and often at the level of each discrete objection within a response (e.g., "overbroad," "unduly burdensome," "not proportional," "privilege," "confidentiality," "vague/ambiguous," "calls for expert opinion," "equally available").

Set a naming convention and unique identifier for each dispute entry that persists across updates—for example, "RFP12-PRIVLOG" for a privilege-log deficiency or "INT7-SPEC" for a deficiency on specificity. This prevents confusion when multiple rounds of supplementation occur. Include a "Notes/Relief Sought" field that frames the requested cure as an order the judge could sign. Illustrative language for this field: "Moving party seeks an order compelling a complete search and production of non-privileged documents responsive to RFP No. 12 for the agreed custodians (A, B, C) for the period January 1, 2022 through December 31, 2023, along with an amended response confirming the search methodology."

Common mistakes include logging disputes only at a high level (e.g., "Deficient RFP responses") instead of by request; failing to preserve the exact language of the objection, which later becomes contested; and failing to capture the narrowed relief sought, leading to overreaching motion requests. Opposing counsel will argue that a deficiency was never clearly articulated or that the requesting party shifted rationales across conferral sessions; designing the tracker to memorialize the exact objection and the responding articulation protects against those accusations.

### Step 2: Create a Legally Grounded Deficiency Taxonomy

Translate the concept of "bad discovery" into legally cognizable deficiency types. This step forces discipline: each deficiency should map to a recognized duty (to respond, to search, to produce, to specify, to verify, to log privilege, to supplement) and to a remedy (amended response, additional production, narrowed request, protective order, cost-shifting, sanctions). In federal practice, the key anchors are the scope and proportionality principles of Rule 26(b)(1), the privilege-disclosure requirements of Rule 26(b)(5), the duty to supplement under Rule 26(e), interrogatory response obligations under Rule 33, production obligations and specificity requirements under Rule 34, and the motion-to-compel and sanctions structure of Rule 37.

High-value deficiency categories include: improper boilerplate objections that fail to state with specificity the grounds for objecting; failure to state whether responsive materials are being withheld on the basis of an objection; failure to specify the part responded to when objecting in part and producing in part; overbreadth or proportionality objections unsupported by specific facts regarding the volume of data, cost of retrieval, or burden; privilege claims without an adequate privilege log as required by Rule 26(b)(5); confidentiality objections where a protective order already exists; non-responsive or evasive answers; incomplete searches (insufficient custodians, date ranges, or data sources); ESI format issues; missing verifications; and late or absent supplementation.

The deficiency category matters because each type drives a different conferral posture and motion strategy. For proportionality objections, the responding party bears the burden of showing specific hardship under the 2015 amendments to Rule 26(b)(1), which identified six proportionality factors; a boilerplate invocation of the word "proportionality" without addressing those factors is insufficient. For privilege objections, the tracker should distinguish between attorney-client privilege and work-product protection because each requires different detail in a privilege log. For "conditional" responses—the common "subject to and without waiving objections, will produce responsive documents" formulation—the deficiency is the ambiguity about what is actually being withheld.

Illustrative deficiency entry language: "Deficiency type: Rule 34(b)(2)(C) specificity. Response objects and produces 'non-privileged documents' but does not state whether documents are withheld on the basis of objection and does not specify whether production is complete as to each category requested." The LLM should write deficiency statements at this level of precision, tying each to the governing rule, and either provide verified rule text with a URL or mark the specific standard as requiring attorney verification.

### Step 3: Populate Each Entry with Primary-Source Quotations and Record Citations

Populate the tracker entry-by-entry using the exact text of the request and the exact text of the response and objections, including definitions and temporal scope. Do not paraphrase. This is critical because discovery disputes often turn on subtle wording—whether a request is limited to a topic, a time period, a set of custodians, or "documents sufficient to show." The tracker must also identify where in the record each element appears: Bates range for productions, PDF page for the response set, date of service and method, and version number if amended.

Precision serves multiple purposes. A court will not accept generalized characterizations like "they refused to produce anything" if the response technically promised a rolling production or produced something minimal. Precision also lets the team detect internal inconsistencies—for instance, an objection claiming "no such documents exist" may conflict with deposition testimony or documents produced in response to other requests.

For each disputed request, include: the request number and full text; the response date, method of service, and full text; whether documents were produced and the Bates range; whether a privilege log was served and its date; and any supplementation dates with a short description of what changed. If the forum will later require a declaration, these data points allow the declarant to testify accurately.

Illustrative quotation practice: "RFP No. 12 (served 2025-01-10): 'Produce all communications between X and Y regarding the Z Project from 1/1/22 to present.' Response (served 2025-02-12): 'Responding party objects that this request is overbroad, unduly burdensome, and not proportional to the needs of the case. Subject to and without waiving objections, responding party will produce responsive, non-privileged documents.' Production: BATES DEF000120–DEF000145 on 2025-03-01."

Common mistakes include omitting the "subject to and without waiving" language, which often drives the deficiency; failing to capture service dates, which control motion deadlines; and mixing multiple requests into one entry, making later narrowing and court reporting difficult. Opposing counsel will use any characterization of their response to argue misquotation; verbatim primary-source entries minimize that risk.

### Step 4: Analyze Each Objection and Craft a Targeted Deficiency Statement with Proposed Cure

For each entry, the tracker must include a deficiency narrative that identifies what is wrong, ties it to a governing standard, and states the minimal and reasonable cure that will be accepted. This is where the tracker becomes an advocacy document: the deficiency statement is the first draft of the argument that may later be made to the court, and the proposed cure should be phrased as a concrete order a judge could sign.

Classify each objection and respond accordingly. If the response asserts overbreadth, burden, or disproportionality, require the opposing party to address specifics: what custodians, what sources, what volume, what time period, what cost, and what alternative narrowing would resolve it. The 2015 amendments to Rule 26(b)(1) placed the proportionality factors within the scope definition itself, making conclusory burden claims legally insufficient. If the response asserts relevance, connect the request to specific claim or defense elements and explain why the information matters to a genuinely disputed issue. If the response asserts privilege, assess whether the claim is plausibly invoked and whether a log or categorical log is required. If the response is the "subject to objections, will produce" conditional form, the key deficiency is that the requesting party cannot determine whether anything is being withheld.

Write deficiency statements as if they will be pasted directly into a meet-and-confer letter. Avoid rhetoric. Use concrete requests and offer reasonable concessions where appropriate—a narrowed timeframe, specific custodians, search terms, or phased production. Where the governing standard depends on case law (such as what constitutes improper boilerplate objections in a given district), the LLM must either pull controlling authority through web search and include only verified citations with URLs, or explicitly mark the authority as requiring attorney verification. Under no circumstances should the LLM fabricate case citations.

Illustrative deficiency statement: "Deficiency: The response asserts boilerplate objections and does not state whether any responsive documents are being withheld on the basis of those objections, as required by Rule 34(b)(2)(C). Proposed cure: Serve an amended response that (1) confirms whether any documents are withheld, (2) identifies the custodians and repositories searched, (3) produces all non-privileged responsive documents, and (4) if withholding on privilege, provides a privilege log sufficient to assess the claim under Rule 26(b)(5)."

Common mistakes include demanding "all documents" without specifying how to operationalize the search; failing to propose a cure a judge can order; ignoring proportionality and thereby appearing unreasonable; and failing to distinguish between "no documents exist" and "we did not search." Opposing counsel will portray demands as harassment or overreach; a narrowly framed cure tied to a concrete litigation need makes that characterization difficult to sustain.

### Step 5: Conduct and Document Meet-and-Confer Cycles with Litigation-Grade Detail

The tracker must treat meet-and-confer as an evidentiary sequence, not a casual email thread. Under FRCP 37(a)(1), the movant must certify that it "has in good faith conferred or attempted to confer" with the opposing party before filing a motion to compel. Many courts interpret this to require a substantive verbal exchange—a phone call or in-person meeting—not merely an exchange of letters or emails. The tracker's conferral log is the foundation for that certification.

For each dispute entry, log every conferral event with date, participants, mode (phone, video, in-person, email), and substance. The key is to capture what was said and what was agreed with enough clarity that a declaration can truthfully state the efforts and outcome. After each substantive conferral, generate a written confirmation to opposing counsel summarizing what issues were resolved, what remains disputed, what each side committed to produce or revise, and dates for next steps. Then update the tracker with fields for "Opposing Justification," "Our Response," "Agreed Narrowing," "Commitments," and "Next Follow-Up Date." Use neutral, accurate language suitable for attachment to a motion as an exhibit.

Illustrative conferral log entry: "3/14/2026 call (Atty Smith / Atty Lee). OC states burden objection is based on 12 custodians and archived Slack exports; agrees to produce from custodians X, Y, and Z for 1/1/22–12/31/23 by 3/28 and to propose search terms for remaining custodians by 4/4. We agree to defer archived sources pending review of initial production."

Common mistakes include logging only "M&C done" without substance; failing to capture who attended, which matters if later challenged; letting weeks pass without follow-up dates; and failing to separate "agreement in principle" from concrete commitments with deadlines. Opposing counsel may later deny agreements or claim they were conditional; a contemporaneous confirmation email and tracker entry neutralize that tactic.

The tracker should include a "Forum Requirement Satisfied?" field (yes/no/partial) with a short explanation, because some courts require telephonic or in-person conferral rather than letters alone, and some require joint submissions that include each side's position verbatim. These requirements must be confirmed against the specific local rules and standing order of the assigned judge.

### Step 6: Maintain Dispute Status, Escalation Readiness, and Deadline Control

The tracker must display a "current status" that supports reporting to the client and, if needed, to the court. Status should be objective and tied to the next required action: "Open—awaiting supplementation by [date]"; "Partially cured—reviewing production"; "Narrowed—only privilege log remains"; "Impasse—ready for IDC"; "Escalated—joint letter due [date]"; "Resolved—no further action." The point is to prevent drift and to ensure each open issue has an owner and a next date.

Deadline control is not optional. Each entry should include at least two deadline types: external deadlines (discovery cut-off, motion cut-off, local rule deadlines to move to compel after service of a response) and internal deadlines (date to follow up, date to draft a letter, date to request a court conference). If the user cannot provide applicable motion-to-compel deadlines, the LLM must instruct them to confirm via local rules and label those tracker deadlines as requiring verification. Many jurisdictions impose short, strict deadlines—for example, some California motion-to-compel provisions are keyed to 45 days after service of the response—and missing them can forfeit the right to compel.

When the team reaches impasse, the tracker should help convert the dispute into a narrow ask for the court. That means storing, in one place, the "Relief Sought" phrased as an order the judge can sign, and the "Narrowing History" showing the reasonable steps taken. Include a "Materiality" note describing how the discovery ties to a claim element, damages theory, class certification, expert analysis, or impeachment. Escalating too broadly—seeking relief on forty requests when only five matter—dilutes credibility. Failing to document narrowing invites the court to view the dispute as unnecessary. Failing to track deadlines while continuing to confer lets the other side run out the clock.

### Step 7: Assess Sanctions Exposure and Build the Record

Throughout the tracking process, evaluate whether the opposition's conduct rises to the level of sanctionable behavior under FRCP 37(a)(5), which provides for an award of reasonable expenses including attorney's fees when a motion to compel is granted, or under FRCP 37(b) for violation of court orders. The tracker should proactively flag requests where the opponent's position is particularly weak or where the same deficiency has persisted across multiple conferral cycles despite clear articulation, suggesting that the attorney consider requesting fees.

This assessment serves a dual purpose: it deters further bad-faith conduct and creates an evidentiary trail showing that costs were incurred because of unreasonable positions. The tracker should record the number of conferral events and the dates of each for each disputed request, making it straightforward to quantify the effort expended.

### Step 8: Generate Reporting Views for Client Updates and Court Submissions

Use the tracker to generate two derivative views without changing the underlying data: a client-facing status report and a court-facing dispute chart.

The client-facing view should summarize, in plain but precise language, what is being disputed, why it matters to the case, and the cost-benefit analysis and timeline for escalation. It should not include inflammatory characterizations like "bad faith" unless counsel has a deliberate strategy for that label and a factual basis to support it. Client reporting helps maintain alignment and avoids surprises about potential motion practice costs.

The court-facing view should be strictly factual, organized request-by-request, and should include only disputes that are genuinely at impasse after good-faith conferral. Where the forum requires joint submissions, the tracker should support capturing the opposing side's position verbatim and the requesting party's response. Where the forum requires a "Separate Statement" (as in California state court under CRC 3.1345), the tracker must produce a self-contained document that reproduces the full text of each request and response rather than incorporating by reference. Where case law is required, the LLM must either provide verified citations with URLs or leave clear placeholders for attorney insertion.

Common mistakes include copying internal strategy commentary into court papers; including resolved issues, which dilutes credibility; and failing to attach key exhibits (the request, the response, key emails) because the tracker did not identify them. Court submissions are often read quickly by judges with heavy dockets; a crisp, narrow chart signals reasonableness and increases the odds of obtaining relief.

## 5. Critical Legal Standards and Compliance

Competent use of this skill requires strict attention to professional responsibility and discovery ethics. Model Rule 1.1 (Competence) and Model Rule 1.3 (Diligence) require counsel to manage discovery disputes proactively, including understanding procedural prerequisites and deadlines. Model Rule 3.2 (Expediting Litigation) is directly implicated: discovery tools must not be used to delay or burden litigation. Model Rule 3.4 (Fairness to Opposing Party and Counsel) applies to both sides of the dispute—an attorney must not make frivolous discovery requests and must not engage in dilatory tactics in responding. Model Rule 3.3 (Candor Toward the Tribunal) and Rule 4.1 (Truthfulness in Statements to Others) require that any court reporting of meet-and-confer efforts and any characterization of the other side's position be accurate and not misleading. Any misstatement of what opposing counsel said or agreed to in the conferral record is a potential candor violation.

Privilege and confidentiality are central. The tracker itself can contain privileged attorney work product (legal theories, strategy, mental impressions) and sensitive client data. Treat the tracker as attorney work product and store it in secure systems consistent with Model Rule 1.6 (Confidentiality of Information) and firm security policies. Do not send the tracker to the client or third parties without considering waiver risks. If the tracker must be shared (e.g., to coordinate with co-counsel under a common-interest agreement), limit distribution, mark it as privileged and confidential work product where appropriate, and segregate purely factual content from strategy commentary.

Malpractice risk areas include: missing motion-to-compel deadlines; inaccurately stating what was requested or what was responded; overreaching or failing to narrow, thereby losing court credibility; making allegations of bad faith without evidentiary support; and failing to preserve issues for later sanctions requests. Mitigate these risks by using primary-source quotations, logging dates and service methods, confirming agreements in writing, and clearly labeling uncertain items for attorney verification.

Statutory and regulatory overlays may apply in particular cases (e.g., HIPAA, FERPA, GDPR, state privacy laws, financial regulations) affecting production, redaction, or protective order terms. The tracker should include a "Regulatory Constraint" note when a withholding or redaction is justified by statute, and should record whether a protective order or data-handling protocol is required. Do not assert a regulatory bar without confirming the governing statute and its applicability through authoritative sources.

## 6. Quality Control and Review

Quality control means testing whether the tracker would survive hostile review by opposing counsel and skeptical review by the court. Apply the following framework before any tracker output is used externally.

First, confirm verbatim accuracy: are requests and responses quoted exactly? Even minor paraphrasing can be used by the opposition to claim the record is distorted. Second, confirm exhaustion of remedies: does the tracker show that every individual request included in a motion was actually discussed during the meet-and-confer? Courts frequently strike items from a motion if they were not specifically raised in the conferral. Third, confirm legal substantiation: does every deficiency statement cite a specific rule? Avoid purely factual arguments; frame them as legal failures. Fourth, confirm formatting compliance: does the output match the local rules of the specific forum—three-column chart, joint letter, or separate statement? Fifth, test for internal consistency: ensure that relief sought is consistent with the narrowing history and that the same request is not listed as both resolved and open in different places.

Sixth, validate all citations and dates. Dates drive deadlines, and mis-entered dates are a silent failure mode. If the tracker includes any legal citations (rules, statutes, cases), the LLM must provide a URL to an authoritative source for each, or mark it for attorney verification. The LLM must not generate plausible-sounding but unverified case citations.

Finally, the tracker must flag uncertainties. If the request text is missing, if the response is incomplete, if the local rule deadline is unknown, or if judge procedures have not been reviewed, the tracker should include an "Attorney Verification Needed" note.

All output produced using this skill requires attorney review before use in any meet-and-confer communication, client report, or court filing. This skill is not legal advice; it is a practice support workflow that must be supervised by licensed counsel.

## 7. Jurisdiction-Specific Adaptations

This skill is designed to be jurisdiction-agnostic at the data-structure level (request, response, objection, deficiency, conferral, status, deadlines), but the legal triggers and escalation paths vary by forum. The LLM executing this skill must ask at the outset: "What court or jurisdiction and what assigned judge?" and must treat escalation procedures and deadlines as unverified until confirmed via authoritative court sources.

In federal court generally, the baseline concepts of proportionality under Rule 26(b)(1), supplementation under Rule 26(e), and motions to compel and sanctions under Rule 37 govern. However, local rules and judge standing orders often impose specific pre-motion steps such as an informal discovery conference, a joint letter with page limits, or a requirement to quote disputed discovery verbatim. Many judges have individual rules that must be checked on the court's website. In busy federal districts such as the Northern District of California, the Southern District of New York, and the District of Delaware, judges typically insist on short, issue-narrowed letters and require that parties propose practical compromises addressing custodians, date ranges, and search terms.

In California state court, discovery is governed by the Civil Discovery Act (Code of Civil Procedure § 2016.010 et seq.) and includes device-specific motion-to-compel provisions and meet-and-confer requirements that differ across interrogatories (§ 2030.300), document demands (§ 2031.310), and requests for admission (§ 2033.290). Deadlines are often statutory and unforgiving. The mandatory Separate Statement format under California Rule of Court 3.1345 requires a self-contained document reproducing the full text of each disputed request and response. The tracker must be adapted to include statutory motion deadlines keyed to the service date and method of service and must capture verifications and statutory response requirements.

In New York state court, discovery practice is shaped by the CPLR and, if applicable, the Commercial Division Rules. Commercial Division Rule 14 requires a discovery conference with the court before any formal motion can be filed, and the tracker should be optimized to serve as the concise letter required by the court to summarize the issues. If in the Commercial Division, the tracker should include fields aligned to the Part Rules and Commercial Division requirements, including pre-motion conference mandates and limitations on motion practice.

In Texas state court, the tracker should address Level 1, 2, or 3 discovery plans and the good-faith requirement under Texas Rule of Civil Procedure 191.2. Texas requires a Certificate of Conference that must state the parties actually talked—or explain why they could not—making the conferral log especially important.

Because these jurisdictional variations are high-stakes, the tracker should include a "Forum Requirement" field for each dispute entry and should not assume any particular local rule or deadline applies without confirmation from an authoritative source.
